name: Halite
visual_profile: Perfect cubic cleavage blocks with glassy luster; water-clear to milky white or pink; dissolves in water leaving cubic pits
formation: Evaporite deposits from evaporation of seawater or saline lakes; hydrothermal veins

name: Diamond
color: Colorless, yellow, blue, pink, green, black
visual_profile: Brilliant adamantine luster with extreme dispersion (fire); octahedral or tetrahedral habit; exceptional hardness; brilliant cut maximizes internal reflection
formation: High pressure-temperature in kimberlite and lamproite pipes; mantle xenoliths; ultra-high-pressure metamorphism

name: Gold
visual_profile: Rich yellow metallic luster; highly malleable and ductile; dendritic or nugget forms; bright specular reflection
formation: Hydrothermal veins (epithermal/mesothermal); placer deposits; gold nuggets in alluvial gravels

name: Copper
visual_profile: Distinctive rose-red metallic color; malleable; tarnishes to malachite-green patina; wire-like or dendritic crystals in native deposits
formation: Oxidized zones of copper sulfide deposits; native in basaltic lavas (Michigan); hydrothermal

name: Iron (alpha)
visual_profile: Steel-grey metallic BCC structure; ferromagnetic at room temperature; transforms to FCC gamma-Fe above 912°C
formation: BCC alpha-iron stable below 912°C; found in iron meteorites; smelted from hematite and magnetite ores

name: Fluorite
color: Purple, green, yellow, blue, colorless, pink, black
visual_profile: Brilliant cubic or octahedral crystals in rainbow colors; perfect four-way octahedral cleavage; fluorescent blue-white under UV; phantoms and color zoning common
formation: Hydrothermal veins; contact metamorphic; carbonatites; accessory in pegmatites

name: Pyrite
visual_profile: Brilliant brass-yellow cubes, octahedra, and pyritohedra; striated cube faces; tarnishes to bronze-brown; forms spectacular crystal clusters
formation: Hydrothermal veins; sedimentary (diagenetic); metamorphic; magmatic; common in many ore deposits

name: Galena
visual_profile: Heavy lead-grey cubes and octahedra with brilliant metallic mirror-like luster; perfect cubic cleavage yields bright flat surfaces; tarnishes dull grey
formation: Hydrothermal Mississippi-Valley type deposits; contact metasomatic; vein deposits with sphalerite

name: Magnetite
visual_profile: Black octahedral crystals with magnetic properties; black streak; metallic to submetallic luster; strongly attracted to magnets
formation: Magmatic segregation; hydrothermal veins; metamorphic (skarn); sedimentary (banded iron formations)

name: Spinel
visual_profile: Brilliant octahedral gem crystals; classically deep red (ruby spinel) or blue; no birefringence; prototype of spinel AB₂O₄ structure type
formation: Contact metamorphism of Al-Mg limestone/dolostone; metamorphic crystalline schists; placer deposits

name: Almandine Garnet
visual_profile: Deep wine-red dodecahedral and trapezohedral crystals; no birefringence; high refractive index; dark red color from Fe²⁺; star garnets show asterism
formation: Regional metamorphism of pelitic rocks; mica schists, gneisses; also in eclogites

name: Silver
visual_profile: Bright white metallic luster; wire, dendritic, or massive habit; tarnishes black (Ag₂S); highest electrical conductivity of all metals
formation: Hydrothermal veins with galena, copper minerals; secondary enrichment zones; native silver associated with arsenides

name: Platinum
visual_profile: Brilliant white-grey metallic luster; dense heavy grains and nuggets; forms placer deposits; highly corrosion resistant
formation: Ultramafic and mafic intrusions (Bushveld, Sudbury); komatiites; placer deposits from erosion

name: Lead
visual_profile: Dull lead-grey soft metal; very high density; leaves grey marks on paper; oxidizes rapidly to white lead oxide on surface
formation: Native lead rare; primarily from galena (PbS) smelting; secondary lead from anglesite, cerussite

name: Nickel
visual_profile: Pale silver-white metallic with slight golden tint; ferromagnetic; forms kamacite and taenite in iron meteorites
formation: Ultramafic rocks (komatiites, peridotites); pentlandite in magmatic Ni-Cu sulfide deposits; meteorite iron-nickel alloy

name: Aluminum
visual_profile: Bright silver-white metallic; lightest structural metal; highly reflective; FCC soft metal; does not occur native — extracted from bauxite/corundum
formation: Does not occur as native element; extracted electrolytically from alumina (Al₂O₃) from bauxite ore

name: Chromite
visual_profile: Black octahedral crystals in peridotite; spinel structure; slightly magnetic; brown streak; refractory mineral stable to high temperatures
formation: Magmatic segregation in ultramafic rocks (dunite, peridotite); podiform chromite in ophiolites; stratiform chromite in layered intrusions (Bushveld)

name: Sphalerite
color: Yellow, brown, black, red, green, colorless
visual_profile: Resinous to adamantine luster in tetrahedral-cubic crystals; color varies widely; cleaves perfectly on six {110} faces; high refractive index; piezoelectric (F4̄3m lacks inversion)
formation: Hydrothermal Mississippi-Valley type; skarn; volcanogenic massive sulfide deposits; with galena

name: Periclase
visual_profile: Rock salt structure with MgO; colorless to white cubic crystals; high melting point (2852°C); important lower mantle mineral (MgSiO₃ + FeO → bridgmanite + ferropericlase)
formation: Contact metamorphism of dolomite; calcination of magnesite; mantle lower mantle phase (ferropericlase)

name: Hauyne
color: Vivid blue, white, grey, yellow, pink
visual_profile: Vivid blue sodalite-group mineral; dodecahedral or octahedral crystals; component of lapis lazuli; strong blue color from S₃⁻ radical anion
formation: Contact metamorphism of limestone by alkali-rich magmas (phonolite, tephrite); Italy (Vesuvius), Germany (Eifel)

name: Quartz
color: Colorless, white, purple (amethyst), yellow (citrine), pink (rose), smoky
visual_profile: Hexagonal prisms with rhombohedral caps; vitreous luster; handedness visible via etch figures; strongly piezoelectric; vast color variety; most common mineral in continental crust
formation: Igneous (granite, pegmatite); hydrothermal veins; sedimentary (sandstone, chert); metamorphic; extremely common in continental rocks

name: Ice Ih
visual_profile: Hexagonal snowflakes with six-fold dendritic branching; hexagonal prisms in glacial ice; clear to blue; hydrogen-bonded O-H network; less dense than liquid water
formation: Crystallization of water below 0°C at atmospheric pressure; snowflakes from atmospheric water vapor; glaciers; polar ice caps

name: Graphite
visual_profile: Layered hexagonal carbon sheets (graphene stacks); perfect basal cleavage; greasy feel; writes on paper; conducts electricity; deepest black mineral
formation: Metamorphism of carbonaceous sediments; contact metamorphism; hydrothermal veins; high-pressure marble

name: Zinc
visual_profile: Bluish-white HCP metal; high c/a ratio (1.856) indicating non-ideal packing; tarnishes to dull grey; granular texture in sphalerite-smelted zinc
formation: Rarely native; primarily from sphalerite (ZnS) smelting; also smithsonite, hemimorphite secondary deposits

name: Beryl
visual_profile: Long hexagonal prismatic crystals; color from trace impurities (Cr→emerald, Fe→aquamarine); ring silicate Si₆O₁₈ units; gemstone of extraordinary clarity
formation: Granite pegmatites; hydrothermal veins; mica schists (Colombian emerald); contact metamorphic

name: Apatite
color: Blue-green, green, yellow, colorless, brown, violet
visual_profile: Hexagonal prismatic crystals; Mohs 5 standard mineral; main phosphate mineral in bone and teeth; common accessory in igneous rocks; fluorapatite most stable variety
formation: Accessory mineral in igneous and metamorphic rocks; sedimentary phosphate deposits; biomineralization in vertebrate bones

name: Molybdenite
visual_profile: Layered MoS₂ with graphite-like structure; ultra-thin layers are indirect bandgap semiconductors; extremely low friction coefficient; blue-grey metallic sheets
formation: Porphyry copper-molybdenum deposits; granite pegmatites; skarn contact zones

name: Wurtzite
visual_profile: Hexagonal ZnS polymorph (vs cubic sphalerite); pyramidal crystals; strongly piezoelectric and pyroelectric (P6₃mc polar); important LED semiconductor structure type
uses: Semiconductor template (ZnO, GaN, AlN have wurtzite structure)
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal conditions; metastable relative to sphalerite below ~1020°C

name: Greenockite
visual_profile: Vivid orange-yellow pyramidal crystals; cadmium analog of wurtzite; bright sulfur-yellow color; rare mineral forming thin coatings on sphalerite
formation: Oxidized zones of zinc deposits; thin coatings on sphalerite and zinc ores; rare secondary mineral

name: Titanium
visual_profile: HCP alpha-titanium (stable <882°C); transforms to BCC beta-Ti above 882°C; extremely corrosion-resistant; high strength-to-weight ratio
formation: Does not occur as native element; extracted from ilmenite and rutile; common in mafic igneous rocks as accessory mineral

name: Magnesium
visual_profile: HCP near-ideal (c/a=1.624); lightest structural metal; burns brilliant white (magnesium flare); corrodes to MgO; never found native in nature
formation: Never found native; extracted from seawater MgCl₂ or magnesite/dolomite ores; fourth most abundant element dissolved in seawater

name: Vanadinite
visual_profile: Brilliant orange-red hexagonal barrel prisms; apatite structure type; forms drusy clusters on limonite matrix; vivid color from vanadate group; heavy and dense
formation: Secondary mineral in oxidized zones of lead deposits; arid desert environments (Morocco, Arizona); with mimetite and pyromorphite

name: Pyromorphite
visual_profile: Bright green hexagonal barrel crystals; heavy Pb phosphate mineral; small druse crystals on limonite; vivid forest green common variety
formation: Secondary mineral in oxidized lead deposits; phosphatic groundwaters react with galena; common with cerussite and anglesite

name: Mimetite
color: Yellow, orange, white, colorless, pale green
visual_profile: Pale yellow to honey-yellow hexagonal crystals; arsenic analog of pyromorphite; forms rounded barrel-shaped druses; heavy mineral; campylite variety is curved
formation: Secondary oxidation zone of lead-arsenic deposits; with anglesite, cerussite, vanadinite; dry arid environments

name: Tridymite
visual_profile: SiO₂ polymorph stable 870–1470°C; often preserved as metastable low-tridymite at room temperature; thin platy crystals in tuffaceous rocks; trillings (characteristic triple twins)
formation: High-temperature crystallization in silicic volcanic rocks (rhyolite, obsidian devitrification); meteorite impact ejecta

name: Zircon
color: Brown, red, yellow, green, colorless, blue
visual_profile: High-dispersion gem with adamantine luster; tetragonal prismatic habit; oldest known mineral on Earth (4.4 Ga Jack Hills); U-Pb geochronometer; metamict varieties from radiation damage
formation: Accessory mineral in granitic rocks; concentrated in heavy mineral sands; extremely resistant to weathering; detrital in ancient sediments

name: Rutile
visual_profile: Deep blood-red to black tetragonal prisms; highest birefringence of common minerals (0.298); needle inclusions in quartz form sagenite; adamantine metallic luster; knee-twins diagnostic
formation: Accessory in metamorphic and igneous rocks; heavy mineral placers; eclogite indicator; often as needles included in quartz

name: Cassiterite
visual_profile: Adamantine heavy tin ore; twin crystals (elbow twins); streaks light brown to white; often striated faces; high density (wood tin variety has botryoidal texture)
formation: Granite pegmatites; hydrothermal greisen and quartz veins; placer (alluvial) cassiterite deposits; with tourmaline, wolframite

name: Chalcopyrite
visual_profile: Brass-yellow primary copper sulfide; often with iridescent blue-purple tarnish (peacock ore); tetrahedral crystals; most important copper ore mineral in porphyry Cu deposits
formation: Porphyry copper deposits; hydrothermal veins; magmatic sulfide; skarn; VMS deposits

name: Scheelite
color: White, yellow, orange, green, brown, colorless
visual_profile: White to yellow pseudo-octahedral crystals; bright blue-white fluorescence under short-wave UV; high density; main tungsten ore mineral; scheelite structure type for many molybdates/tungstates
formation: Skarn contact metasomatic deposits; hydrothermal quartz veins; granite pegmatites; with wolframite

name: White Tin
visual_profile: Distorted BCT lattice (compressed BCC); transforms to grey cubic alpha-Sn below 13.2°C (tin pest); emits cracking sound (tin cry) when bent; used since Bronze Age
formation: Extracted from cassiterite; occurs in alluvial placer deposits; associated with granitic tin provinces

name: Apophyllite
color: Colorless, white, pale green, pink, yellow
luster: Vitreous on prism faces; pearly on basal
visual_profile: Tabular to blocky transparent crystals with vitreous to pearly luster; tetragonal prism; matrix for spectacular zeolite associations in basalt vesicles; soft brilliant crystals
formation: Hydrothermal veins and vesicles in basalt; with zeolites, prehnite, pectolite; classic specimens from Pune (Deccan Traps, India)

name: Wulfenite
color: Bright orange, yellow, red, brown, grey
visual_profile: Brilliant flat orange or yellow square tabular crystals; scheelite structure; high specific gravity; vivid orange color; high refractive index and birefringence; iconic collectors mineral
formation: Secondary mineral in oxidized zones of lead deposits; Red Cloud Mine (Arizona), Tsumeb (Namibia), Los Lamentos (Mexico)

name: Vesuvianite
visual_profile: Complex sorosilicate with tetragonal habit; deep green to brown prismatic crystals; rare blue copper-bearing cyprine variety; skarn indicator mineral; formerly called idocrase
formation: Contact metasomatic skarn; impure limestone/dolomite intruded by granitic magma; with grossular garnet, diopside, wollastonite

name: Indium
visual_profile: Distorted FCC (BCT) metallic structure; softest tetragonal metal; emits high-pitched cry when bent; used in ITO (indium tin oxide) for LCD screens; extremely rare element
formation: Never found native; trace constituent of sphalerite and zinc ores; produced as byproduct of zinc smelting

name: Anatase
visual_profile: Steep pyramidal tetragonal crystals (opposite to rutile); dimorphous with rutile and brookite; photocatalytically active phase; negative uniaxial; metastable at ambient conditions
formation: Hydrothermal low-temperature deposits; Alpine cleft veins; contact metamorphic; secondary alteration of titanite

name: Scapolite
color: Colorless, white, yellow, pink, violet, grey
visual_profile: Prismatic tetragonal crystals similar to feldspar; shows chatoyancy (cat's eye) in fibrous varieties; fluorescent orange-yellow in LW UV; metamorphic/metasomatic indicator
formation: Contact and regional metamorphism; calc-silicate rocks; skarns; hydrothermal replacement of plagioclase

name: Calcite
color: Colorless, white, yellow, pink, green, blue, grey
visual_profile: Extreme birefringence (0.172) — double images visible through clear Iceland spar; perfect rhombohedral cleavage; scalenohedral (dogtooth), rhombohedral (nail-head), prismatic habits; most common carbonate
formation: Most abundant carbonate mineral; limestones, chalks, marbles; hydrothermal veins; cave speleothems (stalactites, stalagmites); biogenic (shells, coral)

name: Corundum
color: Colorless (pure), red (ruby, Cr³⁺), blue (sapphire, Fe+Ti), pink, yellow, green
visual_profile: Barrel-shaped or tabular hexagonal prisms; Mohs 9 standard; six-rayed asterism in star ruby/sapphire from TiO₂ silk inclusions; sapphire blue from Fe²⁺-Ti⁴⁺ intervalence charge transfer
formation: Marble and skarn (Myanmar ruby); gneiss and schist (Sri Lanka sapphire); alluvial placer; synthetic Verneuil or hydrothermal growth

name: Hematite
color: Steel grey to black; earthy red (massive)
visual_profile: Steel-grey specular mirror-like metallic surfaces; red streak always diagnostic; kidney ore botryoidal; red ochre earthy; specularite brilliant silvery plates; main iron oxide mineral
formation: Sedimentary BIF (banded iron formations); hydrothermal; oxidized zones; metamorphic; volcanic fumaroles

name: Schorl (Tourmaline)
color: Black (schorl), pink (rubellite), green (verdelite), blue (indicolite), watermelon bicolor
visual_profile: Elongated trigonal prisms with striated vertical faces; hemimorphic (different terminations); strongly pyroelectric and piezoelectric; triangular cross-section diagnostic; color zoning; boron cyclosilicate
formation: Granite pegmatites (schorl); hydrothermal veins; metamorphic; lithium pegmatites produce gem tourmalines

name: Dolomite
visual_profile: Ordered alternating Ca and Mg layers in calcite structure; curved saddle-shaped rhombohedral faces (baroque dolomite); slightly denser than calcite; does not effervesce in cold HCl (unlike calcite)
formation: Diagenetic replacement of limestone by Mg-rich fluids (dolomitization); hydrothermal veins; metamorphic marbles; origin of massive dolomite controversial (Dolomite problem)

name: Ilmenite
visual_profile: Black tabular or rhombohedral crystals; weakly magnetic; corundum-ilmenite solid solution at high T; primary titanium ore mineral; often intergrown with magnetite (trellis exsolution)
uses: Primary titanium ore (TiO₂ pigment, TiO₂ metal)
formation: Accessory in mafic igneous rocks; magnetite-ilmenite ore bodies; heavy mineral beach sands; exsolution in magnetite

name: Bismuth
color: Silver-white with iridescent pink, yellow, blue tarnish
visual_profile: Silver-white metallic with spectacular iridescent tarnish rainbow; synthetic hopper crystals grow as hollow staircase cubes; most diamagnetic metal; lowest thermal conductivity of metals; unique bismuth hoppers
formation: Hydrothermal veins with cobalt, nickel, silver ores; contact metasomatic; Bolivian tin-silver deposits; synthetic hopper crystals from melt

name: Siderite
visual_profile: Yellow-brown rhombohedral crystals with curved faces; iron carbonate; forms concretionary masses; turns magnetic on heating (Fe₂O₃ exsolution); high birefringence like calcite group
formation: Hydrothermal veins; sedimentary ironstone; siderite concretions in shales; metamorphic; replacing limestone

name: Rhodochrosite
visual_profile: Rose-pink to cherry-red rhombohedral crystals or banded stalactitic masses; manganese carbonate; banded concentric variety makes beautiful cabochons (Argentinian rhodochrosite); sweet-thorn pink color
formation: Hydrothermal veins with silver, lead, copper ores; sedimentary manganese deposits; stalactitic masses in mine workings (Capillitas, Argentina)

name: Smithsonite
color: White, grey, green, blue, yellow, pink, purple
visual_profile: Botryoidal, reniform, and stalactitic masses in vivid colors; rarely well-formed rhombohedra; color from trace impurities (Cu→blue-green, Co→pink, Cd→yellow); often silky to pearly
formation: Secondary oxidation zone of zinc deposits; with sphalerite, galena, cerussite; Kelly Mine (New Mexico) classic specimens

name: Magnesite
visual_profile: White to grey rhombohedral crystals or massive; high MgO content; important refractory mineral; cryptocrystalline variety (bone magnesite) from CO₂ carbonation of serpentinite
formation: Metasomatic replacement of dolomite; carbonation of ultramafic rocks (serpentinite); hydrothermal veins; evaporitic

name: Antimony
visual_profile: Layered semimetal isostructural with bismuth and arsenic; brilliant silver-white metallic; brittle; forms granular or bladed aggregates; stibnite is primary ore (Sb₂S₃)
formation: Hydrothermal veins; found native as reduction product of stibnite; with silver and arsenic minerals

name: Cinnabar
visual_profile: Brilliant scarlet-red trigonal crystals with extreme birefringence (0.343); very heavy; adamantine to resinous luster; chalcopyrite-like tarnish; only significant mercury mineral; toxic
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal deposits near volcanic activity; hot springs; with native mercury, stibnite, pyrite; Almadén Spain, Idrija Slovenia

name: Proustite
visual_profile: Ruby-red prismatic crystals; light-sensitive (darkens with exposure); ruby silver class with pyrargyrite; trigonal with R3c (piezoelectric); extremely high refractive index and birefringence
formation: Hydrothermal low-temperature silver veins; oxidizing zone above higher-temperature silver sulfides; with native silver, argentite, polybasite

name: Sulfur
visual_profile: Bright sulfur-yellow bipyramidal or tabular crystals; extremely high birefringence for organic mineral; resinous luster; burns blue with SO₂; volcanic fumarole deposits; warm to touch (low thermal conductivity)
formation: Volcanic fumarole sublimation; evaporite (caprock of salt domes); bacterial reduction of sulfates; Sicily, Indonesia classic localities

name: Aragonite
color: Colorless, white, yellow, grey, green, blue, pink
visual_profile: Orthorhombic CaCO₃; flos-ferri (branching coral-like); blue cave pearls; hexagonal pseudomorphs (cyclic trilling twins); coral and nacre (mother-of-pearl); denser than calcite; inverts to calcite over geologic time
formation: Biogenic (coral, shells, pearls); cave speleothems; hot spring sinters; metastable below 400 MPa; converts to calcite over time

name: Topaz
visual_profile: Perfect basal cleavage; prismatic crystals with striated faces; Mohs 8 standard mineral; imperial topaz has warm orange-pink color; irradiation produces blue swiss/london blue; heavy for a silicate
formation: Granite pegmatites; hydrothermal quartz veins (greisen); rhyolitic cavities; associated with cassiterite and tourmaline

name: Forsterite
visual_profile: Mg end-member of olivine solid solution; mantle rock-forming mineral; gem variety is peridot (yellowish-green); nesosilicate with isolated SiO₄ tetrahedra; ultramafic igneous indicator
formation: Ultramafic and mafic igneous rocks (dunite, peridotite, basalt); crystallizes from Mg-rich melts; mantle xenoliths; meteorites

name: Barite
color: White, colorless, blue, yellow, red, grey
visual_profile: Tabular crystals or rosette aggregates (desert rose); notably heavy for white mineral (sp. gr. 4.48); cleavage characteristic; widespread in sedimentary and hydrothermal settings; barite rose clusters
formation: Hydrothermal veins; sedimentary (replacement of limestone); residual deposits; hot spring deposits; with galena, sphalerite

name: Marcasite
color: Pale bronze-yellow, tin-white with greenish tint
visual_profile: Orthorhombic dimorphs of pyrite; paler and more yellow-white than pyrite; spear-head and cockscomb twins diagnostic; unstable in moist air (oxidizes to white powder); lower symmetry than cubic pyrite
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal; sedimentary (concretions in shales); unstable — converts to pyrite with time

name: Staurolite
visual_profile: Famous for 60° and 90° cruciform twins (fairy crosses); brown prismatic crystals; common in pelitic metamorphic schists; garnet-staurolite-kyanite zone indicator; pseudo-orthorhombic
formation: Regional metamorphism of pelitic (Al-rich) rocks; mica schists; garnet-staurolite-kyanite assemblage indicates ~550-600°C

name: Brookite
visual_profile: Third TiO₂ polymorph (after rutile and anatase); tabular striated crystals; lower symmetry than rutile; extremely rare as well-formed crystals; metastable photocatalyst
formation: Alpine cleft veins; low-temperature hydrothermal; metamorphic; rare relative to rutile and anatase

name: Enstatite
visual_profile: Orthopyroxene Mg end-member; cleavage at 88° characteristic of pyroxene group; often with bronzite (schiller) variety; common in ultramafic rocks and meteorites; chondritic meteorite component
formation: Ultramafic igneous rocks; pyroxenite, harzburgite, dunite; metamorphic granulites; meteorites (orthopyroxene component)

name: Natrolite
visual_profile: Slender needle-like or acicular crystals; pyroelectric and piezoelectric (polar space group Fdd2); zeolite family; forms silky sprays in basalt vesicles; can absorb and release water reversibly
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal in basalt vesicles and fractures; alteration of nepheline and sodalite; associated with apophyllite, prehnite, calcite

name: Chrysoberyl
color: Yellow-green, green, brown; alexandrite red-green; cat's eye
visual_profile: Alexandrite: color changes red in incandescent, green in daylight (Cr³⁺ absorption); Cat's eye: chatoyancy from parallel silk inclusions; Mohs 8.5 — third hardest natural mineral
formation: Granite pegmatites and mica schists; Be-rich environments; at intersection of mafic and granitic contacts; Ural Mountains, Sri Lanka, Brazil

name: Celestite
color: Pale blue, colorless, white, yellow, orange, red
visual_profile: Pale sky-blue tabular or prismatic crystals; barite group (isostructural); sky blue color; imparts crimson color to fireworks (Sr); classic specimens from Madagascar geodes and Ohio quarries
formation: Sedimentary evaporite deposits; hydrothermal veins; geodes in carbonate rocks; Madagascar classic geode specimens

name: Anhydrite
visual_profile: Dehydrated gypsum (CaSO₄); three perfect cleavages; converts to gypsum by absorbing water (anhydrite → gypsum with 60% volume increase, heaving); harder and denser than gypsum
formation: Evaporite sequences (with halite, gypsum); hydrothermal veins; contact metamorphic; forms below ~42°C under arid conditions

name: Sillimanite
visual_profile: Al₂SiO₅ high-T polymorph; fibrolite variety is silky fibrous (feltlike); forms at highest temperatures of the three polymorphs; chatoyant fibrolite cat's eye; indicator of granulite facies
formation: High-temperature regional metamorphism of Al-rich pelitic rocks; hornfels; granulite facies gneisses

name: Andalusite
color: Pink, red, white, brown, grey; chiastolite has cross inclusion
visual_profile: Al₂SiO₅ low-P polymorph; chiastolite variety has cruciform carbonaceous inclusions (cross pattern on cross-section); pink varieties cut as gems; low-symmetry coordination of Al unusual (5-coordinate in Pnnm)
formation: Low-pressure contact and regional metamorphism of Al-rich pelitic rocks; hornfels aureoles; with cordierite

name: Gypsum
visual_profile: Mohs 2 standard; three varieties: selenite (clear plates), alabaster (fine-grained white), satin spar (fibrous silky); forms 11m giants in Cave of Swords (Naica Mine, Mexico); swallow-tail twins
formation: Evaporite deposits (low-T precipitation from brines); hydrothermal replacement; giant crystals from hydrothermal fluids (Naica, Mexico)

name: Orthoclase
visual_profile: K-feldspar with monoclinic symmetry; perfect cleavage nearly at right angles; Carlsbad twins common; perthite (Na-rich lamellae in K-feldspar); moonstone variety has adularescence (blue glow)
formation: Granitic and syenitic igneous rocks; granite pegmatites; high-grade metamorphic gneisses; Carlsbad twins from slow cooling

name: Azurite
color: Deep azure blue, prussian blue, violet-blue
visual_profile: Vivid deep azure blue — the bluest natural mineral; tabular or prismatic crystals; commonly associated with green malachite; pseudomorphs to malachite; used as blue pigment in Renaissance paintings
formation: Secondary oxidation zone of copper deposits; reacts with CO₂/H₂O and copper; converts to malachite in more oxidized conditions; Arizona, Morocco, Congo classic localities

name: Malachite
color: Bright green, dark green, emerald green
visual_profile: Banded concentric green stalactitic masses; peacock-eye patterns from polished cross-sections; silky fibrous or botryoidal habit; vivid green from Cu²⁺; Ural Mountain malachite used in Russian palatial architecture
formation: Secondary oxidation of copper sulfide deposits; reaction of copper minerals with carbonate-bearing groundwater; Congo/Katanga, Arizona, Ural Mountains classic

name: Diopside
color: White, grey, pale green, dark green, black
visual_profile: Ca-Mg clinopyroxene; chrome-diopside is vivid emerald green (Cr³⁺); violet-diopside (violane) variety; star diopside from India shows 4-rayed asterism; common in marble and skarn
formation: Metamorphic marble and skarn; mafic igneous rocks; mantle xenoliths; Siberia (chrome diopside gem quality)

name: Spodumene
visual_profile: Li-pyroxene; kunzite variety is pink-violet (Mn³⁺); hiddenite is emerald green (Cr³⁺); intensely fluorescent orange in LW UV (kunzite); primary lithium ore mineral in pegmatites
formation: Lithium-rich granite pegmatites; among the largest known crystal; spodumene crystals to 14 meters in South Dakota (Etta Mine)

name: Muscovite
color: Colorless, silvery, pale yellow, pale green, pink
visual_profile: Common mica; perfect basal cleavage into thin elastic sheets; large books in pegmatites once used as window glass (isinglass); sheets used in capacitors; extremely high electrical resistance
formation: Granite pegmatites (large books); metamorphic schists and phyllites; greisen alteration zones

name: Augite
visual_profile: Most common clinopyroxene; dark green-black prismatic crystals in mafic igneous rocks; two cleavages at near-90° angle; hour-glass zoning common in metamorphic augite; basalt phenocryst
formation: Basalt, gabbro, diorite igneous rocks; high-grade metamorphic; most abundant pyroxene in Earth's crust

name: Hornblende
visual_profile: Most common amphibole; double-chain silicate vs pyroxene single-chain; two cleavages at 60/120° (vs pyroxene 87/93°); OH-bearing (amphibolite facies indicator); bladed to prismatic crystals
formation: Intermediate to mafic igneous rocks; amphibolite facies metamorphic; replaces pyroxene during retrograde metamorphism (uralitization)

name: Epidote
visual_profile: Distinctive pistachio green (pistacite); elongated striated prismatic crystals; sorosilicate (Si₂O₇ + SiO₄ groups); indicator of greenschist facies; common in calc-silicate rocks
formation: Greenschist to amphibolite metamorphic rocks; hydrothermal alteration of calc-silicates; skarn; retrograde metamorphism

name: Jadeite
visual_profile: True jade (vs nephrite); interlocking microcrystalline texture gives toughness despite moderate hardness; Imperial jade is vivid emerald green (Cr); waxy translucent luster; high pressure mineral
formation: High-pressure low-temperature metamorphism (subduction zone blueschist); Myanmar (Burma) source of Imperial jade; metasomatic replacement

name: Actinolite
color: Dark green to black, pale green to grey-green
visual_profile: Nephrite jade is massive actinolite-tremolite; fibrous asbestiform variety is amosite/tremolite asbestos; greenschist metamorphic; prismatic to fibrous; forms between tremolite and hornblende in amphibole series
formation: Greenschist metamorphism of mafic rocks; retrograde overprinting; tremolite-actinolite-hornblende series with increasing T

name: Talc
color: White, grey, pale green, pale yellow
visual_profile: Softest mineral (Mohs 1); greasy soapy feel; phyllosilicate sheets held by weak van der Waals forces; perfect basal cleavage; talcum powder; used in cosmetics as slip agent
formation: Low-grade metamorphism of ultramafic rocks (serpentinite); hydrothermal alteration of Mg silicates; talc-carbonate schist

name: Phlogopite
color: Brown, yellow-brown, pale yellow, reddish, colorless
visual_profile: Mg-rich mica; large six-sided books in kimberlite; asterism (six-rayed star) in some varieties; Mg end-member of phlogopite-biotite mica series; high-temperature stability (to 1000°C)
formation: Kimberlite pipes; carbonatites; ultra-high-T metamorphic marbles; Mg-rich skarns; contact aureoles in dolostone

name: Selenite
visual_profile: Clear transparent variety of gypsum; largest known crystals (up to 11 meters, Naica Cave Mexico); fiber optic light transmission; ghost inclusions; double-terminated tabular plates; luminous in moonlight
formation: Hydrothermal fluids below 58°C depositing in anhydrite-bearing caves; Naica Mine Mexico largest known crystals; also Cueva de Villa Luz, Chihuahua

name: Albite
visual_profile: Na end-member feldspar; polysynthetic albite-law twinning gives striations on {010} cleavage face; pearly luster on cleavage; peristerite (blue-white schiller); most common plagioclase at surface
formation: Granite, pegmatite, rhyolite; low-grade metamorphic; hydrothermal; epithermal; most Na-rich plagioclase

name: Anorthite
visual_profile: Ca end-member plagioclase; higher RI and density than albite; primitive cell double that of albite (b-doubled); labradorite intermediate member shows labradorescence (peacock iridescence)
formation: Gabbro, basalt, anorthosite; cumulate rocks; lunar highlands (anorthosite crust)

name: Kyanite
visual_profile: Remarkable hardness anisotropy: H=4.5 parallel to length, H=7 perpendicular; sapphire-blue bladed crystals; Al₂SiO₅ high-pressure polymorph; eclogite-facies indicator; triclinic despite almost orthogonal angles
formation: High-pressure regional metamorphism; eclogite; glaucophane schist; blueschist; with garnet and rutile

name: Turquoise
visual_profile: Waxy sky-blue phosphate; rarely well-crystallized (massive nodules, veins); matrix turquoise shows black/brown veining; Iran and Sinai ancient sources; color fades with dehydration
formation: Secondary mineral in arid copper-bearing desert regions; oxidized zones with malachite and chrysocolla

name: Rhodonite
color: Rose-pink, red, brown; black Mn oxide veins
visual_profile: Rose-pink to cherry-red pyroxenoid; characteristic black Mn oxide veining; carved as ornamental stone; large deposits in Ural Mountains; single-chain silicate like pyroxene but triclinic
formation: Metamorphosed Mn-rich sediments; hydrothermal veins; skarn

name: Axinite
visual_profile: Sharp axe-blade-like crystal habit (hence axinite); pleochroic — color changes with viewing angle; boron-bearing sorosilicate; cleft deposits in Alps; contact-skarn mineral
formation: Contact skarns; alpine clefts; hydrothermal veins in granite contact zones

name: Amblygonite
color: White, pale yellow, pale green, colorless
visual_profile: Lithium aluminum fluorophosphate; often confused with feldspar in hand specimens; greasy luster on some faces; occurs as large masses in Li-rich pegmatites; Li ore mineral
formation: Lithium-rich granite pegmatites; with spodumene, lepidolite, tourmaline

name: Wollastonite
visual_profile: White acicular to fibrous crystals; skarn indicator mineral; used in ceramics as asbestos substitute; near-orthogonal cleavage despite triclinic symmetry; low thermal expansion
formation: Contact metamorphism of limestone (CaCO₃ + SiO₂ → CaSiO₃ + CO₂); skarn; with diopside, garnet, epidote

name: Chalcanthite
visual_profile: Intensely vivid electric blue; water-soluble copper sulfate pentahydrate; short-prismatic tabular crystals; stalactitic in mine drainages; dehydrates to white CuSO₄ in dry air; toxic copper mineral
formation: Secondary mineral in oxidized copper mine drainages; mine walls in dry climates; unstable in humid conditions

name: Microcline
visual_profile: Low-T K-feldspar; cross-hatch (tartan) twinning from combined albite and pericline twins; amazonite variety is vivid blue-green (Pb²⁺ or water causes color); granite pegmatites; slightly triclinic vs monoclinic orthoclase
formation: Slow-cooled granitic pegmatites; low-T metasomatic; ordering from orthoclase with geologic time

name: Opal
color: White, orange, black, red, yellow; play-of-color
visual_profile: Amorphous to poorly crystalline silica with stacked SiO₂ nanospheres producing diffraction play-of-color (opalescence); precious opal has spectral color flashes; hydrated mineral
formation: Low-temperature hydration of silica; cavity fillings in volcanic rocks; Australian sedimentary opal fields

name: Lepidolite
visual_profile: Lavender-purple lithium mica; scintillating in matrix; pegmatite Li-mineral indicator; iridescent book structure; used in Li extraction; rarely transparent gem flakes
formation: Lithium-rich granite pegmatites; with tourmaline, spodumene, topaz

name: Uvarovite Garnet
visual_profile: Rarest and most vivid green garnet; Cr³⁺ gives intense emerald color; crystals usually small (<5mm); forms drusy coatings in chromite; Finland and Ural Mountains classic
formation: Chromite-bearing peridotite; serpentinite; skarn associated with chromite

name: Demantoid Garnet
visual_profile: Andradite variety with highest dispersion of all garnets (0.057); higher dispersion than diamond; Horsetail inclusions of chrysotile unique; brilliant green adamantine luster; Russia (Ural) source
formation: Serpentinite and chlorite schist in Ural Mountains Russia; skarn; metasomatic

name: Amazonite
visual_profile: Blue-green microcline variety; color from Pb²⁺ or water in Al-Si sites; intense turquoise to mint green; used as gem cabochon; ancient Egyptian and Mesoamerican ornamental stone
formation: Pegmatites rich in Pb; Amazon River region; Colorado, Madagascar sources

name: Erythrite
visual_profile: Cobalt bloom (vivid crimson-rose crystalline coatings on cobalt arsenide ores); soft acicular crystals; nicknamed cobalt bloom; prospecting indicator for cobalt deposits beneath
formation: Secondary oxidation of cobalt arsenide minerals (smaltite, cobaltite); thin coatings on host minerals

name: Descloizite
visual_profile: Brownish-black vanadate of Pb and Zn; heavy; druse of small platy crystals; Tsumeb (Namibia) and Arizona sources; related to mottramite (Cu analog); high density for oxide mineral
formation: Secondary oxidation zone of Pb-Zn-V deposits; with vanadinite, wulfenite, malachite

name: Lazurite
visual_profile: Blue mineral component of lapis lazuli; color from S₃⁻ radical anion; sodalite group; deep ultramarine blue; lapis lazuli (lazurite + pyrite + calcite) was most prized blue pigment in history
formation: Contact metamorphism of limestone by Na-rich intrusions; Afghanistan (Sar-e-Sang) classic source; also Russia, Chile

name: Cavansite
visual_profile: Strikingly vivid peacock-teal blue from V⁴⁺; rosette spherical clusters on stilbite matrix; rare and highly prized by collectors; Pune (India) basalt vesicles; blue more intense than aquamarine
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal in basalt vesicles; Deccan Traps India; with stilbite, apophyllite, heulandite zeolites

name: Prehnite
color: Pale green, apple green, yellow-green, white
visual_profile: Apple-green to yellow-green botryoidal masses; first mineral named after a person (Col. van Prehn); reniform and globular habit; forms in low-T basalt cavities and contact zones; sometimes called Cape Emerald
formation: Low-temperature hydrothermal in basalt vesicles; contact metamorphic; zeolite facies

name: Cordierite
visual_profile: Iolite gem variety shows intense blue-violet dichroism (Vikings used as polarizing navigation compass); ring silicate like beryl but orthorhombic; pleochroic from blue to yellow-grey
formation: Low-pressure high-temperature metamorphism; cordierite hornfels; granulites; granites

name: Fayalite
visual_profile: Fe end-member of olivine series; denser and darker than forsterite; nesosilicate; common in granites and iron-rich basalts; metallic-looking brown in hand specimen; partitions Fe from Mg
formation: Iron-rich granitic rocks; iron-rich basalts; meteorites; slag from iron smelting

name: Chrysocolla
visual_profile: Vivid cyan to teal copper silicate; massive botryoidal habit; often coating copper ores; gem silica is precious opal-like blue chalcedony colored by chrysocolla; Arizona copper mines classic
formation: Oxidized zones of copper deposits; secondary with malachite, azurite, cuprite

name: Anglesite
visual_profile: Lead sulfate; adamantine luster with high refractive index; typically massive coating on galena; faceted gems are too heavy and soft for wear; very high density; barite group
formation: Secondary oxidation of galena in Pb deposits; supergene enrichment; with cerussite, smithsonite

name: Minium
visual_profile: Brilliant vermilion-red lead oxide; historically used as red pigment; forms as oxidation crust on galena; extremely heavy; powder-like earthy habit; used as anticorrosion primer (red lead paint)
formation: Secondary oxidation of galena and other lead minerals; rare in nature; historically synthesized

name: Tantalite
visual_profile: Very heavy black columbite-tantalite series (coltan); primary tantalum ore; short prismatic crystals; used in electronic capacitors; conflict mineral from Congo region
formation: Granite pegmatites; alluvial placers; with spodumene, beryl, cassiterite

name: Natrojarosite
visual_profile: Alunite supergroup sulfate; found on Mars (Meridiani Planum); forms at low pH acidic oxidizing conditions; ochre-yellow crust; indicator of acid sulfate weathering
formation: Acid sulfate weathering of iron sulfides; mine drainage; Mars surface (Opportunity Rover identified jarosite)

name: Emerald
color: Deep green to vivid emerald green
visual_profile: Chromium-bearing beryl; vivid green from Cr³⁺ (same ion as ruby — different host); characteristic jardin (garden) inclusions; Colombian emeralds most prized; trapiche emerald shows six-fold sector
formation: Colombia: black shale-hosted hydrothermal veins; Zambia: metamorphic phlogopite schists; Egypt (Cleopatra's mines): mica schists

name: Paraiba Tourmaline
color: Neon blue, electric cyan, vivid blue-green
visual_profile: Copper-bearing tourmaline with extraordinary neon electric blue from Cu²⁺ and Mn; glows from within; most valuable tourmaline variety; Brazil (Paraíba state) discovered 1987; Nigeria and Mozambique also produce
formation: Elbaite pegmatites in Paraíba Brazil; Cu-Mn-bearing granitic pegmatites; recently found Nigeria and Mozambique

name: Wolframite
visual_profile: Primary tungsten ore; tabular to prismatic black crystals; submetallic luster; between ferberite (Fe end) and huebnerite (Mn end); heavy; from medieval German miners (tungsten = heavy stone)
uses: Primary tungsten ore (cutting tools, bulb filaments)
formation: High-T hydrothermal quartz veins; granite greisen; pegmatites; with cassiterite, scheelite

name: Bridgmanite
visual_profile: Most abundant mineral on Earth by mass (lower mantle 660–2900 km depth); perovskite structure; stable above 23 GPa; rarely found at surface (shock veins in meteorites); named 2014 after Nobel laureate Percy Bridgman
formation: Earth lower mantle 660–2900 km depth; impact shock veins in meteorites (Tenham L6); high-P experiments
